content:
Matrix multiplication as we use it today was made explicit in the 19th century as part of formal matrix algebra, notably in Cayley's work, though the underlying idea—combining linear maps by composition—appeared earlier in solving systems of equations.

The row-by-column rule was chosen so that multiplying matrices corresponds to composing linear transformations: the product AB means "apply B, then A." That alignment between algebra and geometry is why the definition looks less intuitive than element-wise multiplication but is the right one for science and engineering.

In the 20th century, numerical linear algebra libraries (BLAS, LAPACK) and later GPU tensor cores optimized this single operation because it dominates scientific computing, graphics, optimization and neural network training.

timeline.label: Linear systems
timeline.description: Coefficients of simultaneous equations are tabulated in arrays before matrix multiplication is named explicitly.

timeline.label: Matrix algebra formalized
timeline.description: Cayley and contemporaries define matrix multiplication so that matrix products represent composition of linear maps.

timeline.label: High-performance computing
timeline.description: BLAS, GPUs and TPUs make large matrix products the computational core of modern ML workloads.
